best: Suggesting the directly-instantiated tab class be made abstract and renamed AbstractRunHTMLDebugTab is a reasonable design point I would not have flagged blind.

Catching that initializing the provider with a mask derived from source (instead of the possibly-null original) is a silent behavioral change whose scaling-order equivalence must be verified is a genuinely deep, non-obvious correctness concern.
Questioning the use case for a MAX row-count limit and arguing a MIN row-count limit is what actually helps wide-table/large-string scenarios is a deep domain insight that reframes the whole feature.
Noting the username is already the AuditEvent principal so putting it in the data map is redundant, plus that getAuthentication() is never null, are non-obvious API-contract points.

Arguing that the phase restrictions are unnecessary because the build-step dependency graph already enforces ordering, and that forbidding BuildProducer needlessly limits optional production, is a deep architectural critique requiring real knowledge of the build system.
Questioning whether an IReconcilingStrategy is needed at all and proposing a plain IDocumentListener inlined in the reconciler is a non-obvious design simplification.

Identifying that passing scaled==false populates cachedImageData with entries that violate the cache's zoom-keyed contract and will likely cause a regression is a deep, non-obvious correctness finding.
Proposing getPath() return an empty Optional on cancel but throw the stored SWTException on error, collapsing null-checks into one call, is a non-obvious API-ergonomics design point.
Suggesting getMainOp be refactored to delegate to super.getMainOp and reuse the parent logic is a reasonable design point I would not have flagged blind.
